data.title: Tesla revenue tied to Musk companies tops half a billion last year
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) generated over $500M in revenue last year from transactions with companies tied to CEO Elon Musk, according to an amended annual filing.

The bulk of the revenue, about $430.1M, came from dealings with xAI (X.AI), which Tesla had initially disclosed in January. In the updated filing released Thursday, the company added another $143.3M in revenue from SpaceX (SPACE), primarily for the sale of vehicles.

Tesla didn’t provide the latter figure in its initial annual filing released in January.

The cross-company activity has continued into 2026. Tesla said it recognized roughly $78.1M in revenue from xAI through February 2026, primarily for the sale of its Megapack products, as well as approximately $0.1M in revenue from SpaceX.

The disclosures come as links between Musk’s businesses deepen. xAI has been purchasing Tesla’s Megapack energy storage systems, while Tesla has begun integrating xAI’s Grok chatbot into its vehicles. Musk has also outlined plans for Tesla and SpaceX to collaborate on chip manufacturing.

SpaceX acquired xAI in February, bringing rockets, satellites, AI and plans for space-based data centers under one roof.

data.title: Corcept raises 2026 revenue guidance to $950M-$1.05B as it targets $2B Cushing’s sales and $1B+ Lifyorli by decade-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was $164.9 million... We have increased our 2026 revenue guidance to $950 million to $1.05 billion. Net loss was $31.8 million... Our cash and investments at March 31 were $515 million.” (CFO & Treasurer Atabak Mokari)
  • Sean Maduck said demand rose with “a record number of new prescriptions written from a record number of prescribers,” and highlighted a near-term revenue timing lag from “onerous reauthorization procedures,” early-stage patient ramp, and “the insurance prior authorization backlog” tied to the specialty pharmacy transition.
  • Roberto Vieira framed the quarter around the FDA approval and early launch indicators for Lifyorli, emphasizing ROSELLA’s survival results and launch execution: “36 days into our launch, things are going very well... Prescriptions have already been written by over 200 physicians... Lifyorli's inclusion in the National Comprehensive Cancer Network... guidelines as a preferred regimen just 15 days after approval will support strong adoption and payer access.” (President of Oncology Vieira)
  • “In April, we met with the FDA regarding relacorilant's new drug application... We will provide an update on relacorilant's regulatory path in the near future.” (Co-Founder, President, CEO & Director Joseph K. Belanoff)

Outlook

  • Mokari attributed the higher full-year range to the combined read-through from both franchises: “our endocrine business represents the bulk of our guidance range just given where we are with oncology,” while citing oncology updates including “approval,” “final ROSELLA results in The Lancet,” and “inclusion in the NCCN guidelines,” and concluding, “we are confidently raising our guidance range.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • Management reiterated long-term revenue ambitions on both pillars: “We expect our current Cushing's Syndrome business to grow to at least $2 billion in annual revenue by the end of this decade.” (President of Endocrinology Sean Maduck) and “We expect Lifyorli only to exceed $1 billion in annual revenue in the United States by the end of the decade.” (President of Oncology Vieira)

Financial Results

  • “Our revenue in the first quarter of 2026 was $164.9 million compared to $157.2 million in the prior year period.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • “Net loss was $31.8 million in the first quarter of 2026 compared to net income of $20.5 million in the first quarter of last year.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • Mokari tied the raised 2026 revenue outlook to operating momentum across divisions: “given the strength on both sides,” management said it expects the endocrinology fundamentals and the new oncology approval dynamics to translate into revenue.

Q&A

  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler & Co.: With updated guidance, is it mostly relacorilant and have Korlym assumptions changed?; CFO Mokari: “our endocrine business represents the bulk of our guidance range... only a small portion... comes from our oncology business,” adding the raise reflects oncology milestones and strong Cushing’s fundamentals.
  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler & Co.: Positioning vs. KEYTRUDA?; President of Oncology Vieira: “we only compete in a subset of the market,” citing KEYTRUDA’s PD-L1 use and saying physicians show “a preference... to actually look into the ROSELLA regimen... as being a preferred regimen.”
  • David Amsellem, Piper Sandler & Co.: How does nenocorilant differ from relacorilant?; Chief Development Officer William Guyer: “nenocorilant has shown strong activity in animal models in combination with immunotherapy,” and the Phase I study is intended to guide “rapidly move forward into a Phase II study.”
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: What share could Lifyorli take with NCCN preferred status?; President of Oncology Vieira: Corcept is “targeting 5,000 physicians” and said it has “every expectation” of adoption, adding, “we do have an expectation of becoming market leader in a relatively short time frame.”
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: Phase III DAZALS design/dose/endpoints/timing?; CDO Guyer: “the endpoint would be survival and the focus would be 300 milligrams,” and the team expects to “start this trial this year and enroll patients by the end of this year.”
  • Swayampakula Ramakanth, H.C. Wainwright: Add another specialty pharmacy?; President of Endocrinology Maduck: “our plan is down the road to expand our network,” and “our plan is to bring in some additional support in the fourth quarter this year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was probing and execution-focused, with repeated questions on guidance drivers, competitive positioning (KEYTRUDA), launch share, and operational capacity (specialty pharmacy).
  • Management tone was confident and promotional around approvals and studies, using phrases such as “we are confident” and “things are going very well,” while providing specific near-term operational actions (e.g., adding pharmacy network support in Q4).
  • Versus the prior quarter, Q1 discussion was less centered on regulatory surprise and more centered on post-approval launch execution and raised revenue guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest shift from Q4 to Q1 was the transition from anticipating an oncology PDUFA decision to reporting an FDA approval: “The FDA's approval of Lifyorli... 3.5 months ahead of its PDUFA date.” (President of Oncology Vieira)
  • Revenue guidance moved higher from Q4’s “$900 million to $1 billion” to Q1’s “$950 million to $1.05 billion.” (CFO & Treasurer Mokari)
  • Q4 emphasized relacorilant’s Cushing’s complete response letter and next steps; Q1 emphasized an FDA meeting on relacorilant’s NDA and said, “We will provide an update on relacorilant's regulatory path in the near future.” (CEO Belanoff)
  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4’s CRL and Korlym pricing dynamics toward Q1’s launch uptake, competitive framing, and pipeline differentiation (nenocorilant) alongside operational scaling.

Risks and Concerns

  • Maduck cited revenue headwinds from payer processes and execution friction: “insurance companies impose onerous reauthorization procedures... that interrupt patient coverage,” and the new pharmacy must work through “the insurance prior authorization backlog.”
  • Belanoff highlighted the need for regulatory clarity on relacorilant in Cushing’s syndrome, stating the company will update investors after FDA interactions.
  • For ALS development, Belanoff said DAZALS discontinuations were driven by tolerability and mitigation is in progress: “Nonserious GI distress caused most of the discontinuations... we think can be avoided,” with a dose-titration study informing the planned pivotal trial.

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 as a quarter of raised full-year revenue guidance, accelerating endocrinology demand signals that are expected to flow through as payer and pharmacy friction clears, and a faster-than-expected oncology milestone with Lifyorli approved ahead of the PDUFA date and showing early breadth of prescribing. Leadership said it will update the market “in the near future” on relacorilant’s Cushing’s regulatory path, while advancing multiple catalysts with timelines including BELLA’s first-arm results by year-end, MONARCH (MASH) data by year-end, and a Phase III ALS study start and enrollment expected later this year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SPS Commerce projects 2026 revenue of $796.0M-$802.0M as Amazon revenue recovery headwinds persist
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SPS Commerce (SPSC) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "SPS Commerce delivered a solid first quarter" and "Q1 revenue grew 6% to $192.1 million," with "recurring revenue" up "7%, driven by fulfillment growth of 8%," according to (CEO & Director Chad Collins).

  • "Tariffs, geopolitics and risk mitigation are fundamentally restructuring global trade" and "SPS is uniquely positioned to deliver the AI-optimized automation trading partners need at scale," Collins said, while adding the company is "introducing a subscription platform fee" for certain Amazon-related revenue recovery customers.

  • Collins highlighted MAX (the company’s AI agent) as a driver of customer ROI and internal efficiency, saying "based on feedback from more than 400 MAX beta customers, the biggest impact AI can have" is "identifying issues early," and that internal agentic efforts are "reducing onboarding and setup time from weeks to days."

  • "To improve profitability across our smaller customer cohorts, we are in the process of introducing a subscription platform fee to our 3P take rate customers" and "we expect this change to increase churn within this cohort with a projected decline of up to 4,000 3P suppliers in 2026," said (Executive VP & CFO Joseph Del Preto), adding, "We do not anticipate this action to result in a material impact to revenue."

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, we expect revenue to be in the range of $194.5 million to $196.5 million" and "we expect fully diluted earnings per share to be in the range of $0.53 to $0.56" while "non-GAAP diluted income per share" is expected to be "$1.06 to $1.09," Del Preto said.

  • "For the full year 2026, we expect revenue to be in the range of $796.0 million to $802.0 million" and "we expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $262.8 million to $267.3 million," with "fully diluted earnings per share" expected at "$2.66 to $2.69" and "non-GAAP diluted income per share" at "$4.73 to $4.76," Del Preto said.

  • Management framed three drivers for the year: "headwinds impacting the Amazon revenue recovery business," revenue recovery ex-Amazon that "continue[s] to outpace overall company growth," and a core business (excluding revenue recovery) that should perform "in line with our expectations," Del Preto said.

  • Versus the prior quarter’s full-year 2026 outlook, management updated ranges to "$796.0 million to $802.0 million" (from "$798.5 million to $806.9 million") for revenue and to "$262.8 million to $267.3 million" (from "$261 million to $265.5 million") for adjusted EBITDA.

Financial results

  • "Revenue was $192.1 million" and "adjusted EBITDA increased to $57.9 million," Del Preto reported.

  • Customer and unit metrics were described as: "The total number of recurring revenue customers in Q1 was approximately 54,200," with "the number of 1P customers" that "was flat sequentially" and "the number of 3P customers declined by 400"; "ARPU was approximately $13,550," Del Preto said.

  • Capital return remained a focus, with Del Preto stating, "In Q1 2026, we deployed nearly 100% of free cash flow to repurchase $47.1 million of SPS shares," and "we ended the quarter with total cash and cash equivalents of $154 million."

Q&A

  • Ian Black, Needham: When does Amazon/3P revenue recovery "start to trough"? Executive VP & CFO Del Preto: "It probably troughs somewhere in the middle of this year, towards the end of this year" and "as we enter into 2027, we would probably see a little bit more momentum."

  • Ian Black, Needham: Update on delayed enablement campaigns? CEO Collins: "Some of the specific campaigns that we cited in Q4" have "either closed or are near closure," but customer count impact is expected to be "more impactful in the second half of the year versus the first half."

  • J. Lane, Stifel: Could Middle East conflict extend the tariff/macro recovery timeline beyond 2Q? CEO Collins: "Not yet seeing any indicators on that" and management is "not hearing that type of thing from our customers at this point in time."

  • J. Lane, Stifel: Why could 4,000 3P customers churn and what cohort is it? CEO Collins: "These are the very smallest of our 3P take rate only Amazon customers" and the new fee is "quite modest at $19.99 a month."

  • Dylan Becker, William Blair: MAX takeaways and custom vs. prebuilt agents? CEO Collins: "We have 400 customers in the beta now" and MAX Connect is "an MCP endpoint" enabling "agent-to-agent interaction" using SPS network and retailer-requirements data.

  • Christopher Quintero, Morgan Stanley: Is high single-digit growth still the medium-term framework and how to get there? CEO Collins: "We do believe that the high single digits over the mid- to long term is the appropriate growth rate," with the "headwind" "very specifically" in "this Amazon revenue recovery piece."

  • George Kurosawa, Citi: Any change in guidance conservatism; and timing of 3P fee rollout/churn? CFO Del Preto: "there's no major change in our guidance philosophy"; CEO Collins: rollout begins "into Q2" and goes "into Q3 a little bit," while churn "may come kind of throughout the year."

  • Joseph Vruwink, Baird: Clarify churn vs. revenue impact and guide pressure. CEO Collins: the 4,000 churn is "not that material to revenue" because revenue per account is "quite modest," and guide pressure is from Amazon policy changes that "reduced the amount of revenue that we're able to recover."

  • Matthew VanVliet, Cantor: AI-driven development velocity and ARPU expansion areas; and M&A vs. buybacks. CEO Collins cited revenue recovery retailer expansion, analytics enhancements, and ERP connections (including NetSuite), while CFO Del Preto said the capital focus is "buying stock back" and the board has authorized "up to $300 million" in repurchases.

  • Nehal Chokshi, Northland: What drives implied back-half growth inflection? CEO Collins: "We're sort of lapping some of the negative effects we had in 2025" and "that will lead to a reacceleration in the back half of '26," while CFO Del Preto said management would "stick with the annual guide."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to neutral, centering on duration of Amazon revenue recovery weakness ("start to trough"), growth deceleration in Q2 ("guiding Q2 to 4% to 5%"), guidance approach ("a few quarters in a row" near "lower end"), and whether back-half inflection assumptions hold.

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A; Collins said "we're not hearing that type of thing from our customers" on new geopolitical disruption and reiterated "high single digits" as the mid- to long-term framework, while also emphasizing the Amazon-related headwind remains "on a negative trajectory" (CFO Del Preto).

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from celebrating "100th consecutive quarter of revenue growth" and newly introduced MAX to more frequent discussion of "headwinds" tied to Amazon revenue recovery and actions to reprice/reshape the smallest 3P cohort.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The CFO transition moved from planned to effective: Q4 introduced a CFO succession with Kimberly Nelson’s retirement timeline and Joseph Del Preto starting March 16; Q1 marked Del Preto’s "first earnings call as SPS CFO."

  • Guidance language placed more weight on Amazon revenue recovery in Q1, with Del Preto saying "we wanted to make sure that we were factoring all the risks we're seeing" in that business; Q4 discussed revenue recovery softness and Amazon policy changes, but did not outline a new 3P subscription platform fee.

  • Revenue growth decelerated sequentially from Q4 2025 revenue of $192.7 million to Q1 2026 revenue of $192.1 million, alongside a year-over-year growth rate moving from 13% in Q4 to 6% in Q1, as described in the respective prepared remarks.

Risks and concerns

  • Management reiterated pressure from Amazon-related policy shifts, with Collins stating the guidance reduction is "driven" by "policy changes that Amazon has made" and Del Preto describing the trajectory as continuing "on a negative trajectory."

  • The company flagged customer pricing scrutiny tied to macro and tariffs; Collins said "we are cautious" on renewals and that the prior "invoice scrutiny" from tariff and macro headwinds showed "early indications" of subsiding.

  • The planned 3P platform fee introduces a churn risk: Del Preto projected a decline of "up to 4,000 3P suppliers in 2026," while Collins described the impacted cohort as "very, very small revenue customers" with a "$19.99 a month" fee.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as solid with $192.1 million in revenue and highlighted AI (MAX and MAX Connect) as a product and internal-efficiency lever, while emphasizing that Amazon-related revenue recovery remains the primary near-term drag. The company guided Q2 revenue to $194.5 million-$196.5 million and maintained a full-year revenue range of $796.0 million-$802.0 million, alongside adjusted EBITDA of $262.8 million-$267.3 million, while introducing a $19.99/month subscription platform fee for the smallest Amazon 3P take-rate customers and projecting up to 4,000 of those suppliers may churn in 2026 without expecting a material revenue impact.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Linde Non-GAAP EPS of $4.33 beats by $0.06, revenue of $8.8B beats by $200M
data.description:
• Linde press release (LIN): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $4.33 beats by $0.06.
  • Revenue of $8.8B (+8.6% Y/Y) beats by $200M.
  • For the full year 2026, the company expects adjusted diluted earnings per share to be in the range of $17.60 to $17.90 (vs. consensus of $17.84), up 7% to 9%, assuming favorable currency of 1%. Full-year capital expenditures are expected to be in the range of $5.0 billion to $5.5 billion to support growth and maintenance requirements including the $7.1 billion contractual sale of gas project backlog.

data.title: SPX Technologies raises 2026 adjusted EPS guidance midpoint to $7.95 amid Section 232 tariff headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SPX Technologies (SPXC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • SPX opened the year with raised full-year expectations after what management described as a strong quarter. “We had a strong start to the year with year-over-year growth in adjusted EBITDA of 23% and adjusted EPS of 22%,” said (President, CEO & Director Eugene Lowe), adding, “We are raising our full year guidance range to reflect our strong performance in Q1 and outlook for the remainder of the year, partially offset by the impact of the recent changes to the Section 232 tariffs.”

  • Management reiterated the tariff timing and duration expectations. “We do not expect these tariffs to impact 2027 earnings,” said (CEO Lowe), while emphasizing ongoing M&A capacity: “Looking ahead, we remain well positioned to continue executing on our organic and inorganic value creation initiatives supported by our robust M&A pipeline.”

  • Capacity and product initiatives were framed as key to meeting data center and custom HVAC demand. “The capacity expansions across our HVAC facilities to meet the strong demand for our data center cooling and custom air handling solutions are progressing well,” said (CEO Lowe). He also noted operational milestones: “In Q1, we began producing highly engineered aluminum dampers in TAMCO's new Tennessee facility,” and “We also began production of the OlympusMAX in our Olathe, Kansas facility in the first quarter.”

  • The company highlighted product/software expansion in Detection & Measurement. “Our location and inspection platform, recently launched a new locate performance management software that meaningfully expands the real-time analysis of our customers' critical data,” said (CEO Lowe).

  • Financial performance and balance sheet flexibility were emphasized. “Year-over-year, adjusted EPS grew by 22% to $1.69,” said (VP, CFO & Treasurer Mark Carano), and “Our leverage ratio... was approximately 0.9x quarter end, below our long-term target range of 1.5 to 2.5x, giving us significant capacity to pursue accretive growth opportunities.”

Outlook

  • SPX increased full-year adjusted EPS guidance. “We are increasing our adjusted EPS guidance by $0.15 to a midpoint of $7.95,” said (CFO Carano), citing “strong Q1 results, particularly in D&M, and additional data center-related volume anticipated to be delivered in the second half of this year.”

  • Section 232 tariffs were quantified and localized in time and segment impact. “Our updated guidance reflects a $0.05 to $0.10 impact from the recently announced changes to the Section 232 tariffs,” said (CFO Carano), adding, “This headwind is expected to predominantly affect HVAC in the second quarter.”

  • Management characterized first-half cadence with an adjustment for tariffs. “Excluding this tariff headwind in Q2, we expect first half adjusted EPS gating to be similar to the prior year,” said (CFO Carano).

Financial Results

  • The quarter’s top-line and segment profitability were driven by acquisitions and HVAC demand, with notable backlog expansion tied to data centers. “For the quarter, total company revenue increased 17.4% year-over-year,” said (CFO Carano), and “Consolidated segment income grew by $25 million or 22% to $135 million.”

  • HVAC performance included both organic and inorganic components, with margin pressure tied to ramp costs. “On an organic basis, revenue increased 9.6%,” said (CFO Carano), while “segment margin decreased 40 basis points, largely due to start-up costs associated with the capacity expansions.” He also said HVAC “segment backlog at quarter end was $755 million, up 38% organically year-over-year, primarily driven by data center demand.”

  • Detection & Measurement margins benefited from software mix. “On an organic basis, revenue increased 3%,” said (CFO Carano), while “segment margin increased 410 basis points,” driven by “greater-than-typical high-margin software volume.”

  • Cash and capital deployment updates included divestiture proceeds and free cash flow. “We ended Q1 with $158 million of cash on hand and total debt of $674 million,” said (CFO Carano). “Q1 adjusted free cash flow was approximately $16 million,” and “we received approximately $60 million in cash proceeds following the completion of the sale of Crawford United's Industrial and Transportation products business.”

Q&A

  • Andrew Obin, BofA Securities: Asked which HVAC end markets were strongest beyond data centers; (CEO Lowe) said data center growth outlook moved “from the neighborhood of 50% to 70%,” with strength in “health care and pharma,” “power,” “heavy industrial,” and “the aftermarket,” while softness persisted in “commercial real estate,” “hotels,” and “battery and semiconductor.”

  • Joseph O’Dea, Wells Fargo: Pressed on HVAC backlog durability and shipping timing; (CFO Carano) pointed to data center orders “going into backlog for 2026” and opportunities for 2027, and (CEO Lowe) added, “We actually see some attractive runway looking ahead in '27 and '28.”

  • Joseph O’Dea, Wells Fargo: Asked about tariff mitigation and reshoring; (CFO Carano) said tariffs were “$10 million of kind of gross costs” with an expectation to “offset... 50%... primarily through price,” and described moving some manufacturing into the U.S. supported by “the TAMCO expansion... in Tennessee and then the Madison facility.”

  • Bradley Hewitt, Wolfe Research: Questioned HVAC margin pressure from capacity ramps; (CFO Carano) referenced “$8 million to $9 million of start-up costs,” with “2/3 of it... in Q1 and Q2,” and said Q1 margin performance “was on track with where we expected it to be.”

  • Jamie Cook, Truist: Asked about normalized incremental margins exiting 2026 and macro demand; (CFO Carano) said, “I'm very confident in our ability to... deliver our traditional kind of incremental margins,” and (CEO Lowe) said bookings were “a little bit ahead about where we thought we would be.”

  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: Asked about radio detection momentum and product impact; (CEO Lowe) said radio detection was “performing very well to date,” and (CFO Carano) stated the business was expected to deliver “mid-single-digit growth.”

  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: Requested acquisition integration and M&A pipeline update; (CEO Lowe) said, “we're very pleased with both of these acquisitions,” described Thermolec channel synergies, and added, “the pipeline is very robust.”

  • Amit Mehrotra, UBS: Sought Q2 calibration and data center capacity unlock; (CFO Carano) said, “HVAC revenue... [is] up sequentially,” and reiterated capacity expansions supporting “circa $550 million of revenue in the data center market,” with Olathe “at full capacity as we get into mid-2027.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment appeared slightly skeptical but engaged, centering on durability of data center demand, backlog timing, margins under capacity ramps, and tariff/inflation exposure; examples included questions on “the step-up in the HVAC orders,” “how you're thinking about the second quarter,” and whether valuations are “spiraling higher.”

  • Management tone was confident in prepared remarks and generally steady in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing visibility and mitigation levers, including “We do not expect these tariffs to impact 2027 earnings” (CEO Lowe) and “I'm very confident” on incremental margins (CFO Carano).

  • Versus the prior quarter, tone remained confident but with more explicit discussion of near-term tariff headwinds and a more specific data center growth uplift (“50% to 70%”) paired with clarified capacity timing.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance moved higher in Q1 2026, as (CFO Carano) raised adjusted EPS guidance “by $0.15 to a midpoint of $7.95,” while Q4 2025 introduced initial 2026 adjusted EPS guidance of “$7.60 to $8.”

  • Data center commentary intensified, shifting from Q4’s expectation of “probably in the 50% neighborhood” growth in 2026 (CEO Lowe, prior call) to Q1’s statement that the outlook moved “from the neighborhood of 50% to 70%” (CEO Lowe, current call), with more emphasis on acceleration and multi-year runway.

  • Tariffs became a more defined near-term modeling factor in Q1, with management now quantifying an adjusted EPS impact of “$0.05 to $0.10” (CFO Carano), whereas Q4 framed tariffs as something to “keep our eye on” without a quantified earnings impact.

  • Detection & Measurement shifted from Q4’s focus on a 2026 growth headwind from a “$20 million” project pull-forward (prior call) to Q1’s discussion of upside margin dynamics tied to “expanded scope” and “high-margin software” (CFO Carano, current call).

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff exposure and timing was the most explicit near-term risk. “Probably 75%, 80% of that is going to fall within the second quarter of this year,” said (CFO Carano), describing mitigations through pricing and other levers.

  • Capacity ramp execution risk was acknowledged through start-up costs and potential inefficiencies. (CFO Carano) cited “$8 million to $9 million of start-up costs” concentrated in the first half.

  • End-market softness persisted in specific HVAC verticals. (CEO Lowe) said “commercial real estate still remains at a relatively low level” and referenced continued softness in “hotels,” with battery and semiconductor still “lower recently.”

Final Takeaway

SPX management characterized Q1 2026 as a strong start, raised its full-year adjusted EPS outlook to a midpoint of $7.95, and positioned data center-related HVAC demand as accelerating, while outlining a near-term Q2 earnings headwind of $0.05 to $0.10 from Section 232 tariffs that it expects to mitigate and not see in 2027. The call emphasized expanding capacity across multiple facilities, margin dynamics tied to ramp costs, and continued balance-sheet flexibility for acquisitions, alongside D&M margin upside linked to higher-margin software scope on existing projects.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Senators ban themselves from betting in prediction markets
data.description:
The U.S. Senate has passed a bipartisan resolution that bans senators from making bets on prediction markets like Kalshi (KALSHI) and Polymarket (POLYMARKET) amid mounting insider trading concerns.

The ban was passed unanimously and is effective immediately. The measure applies to senators, officers and staff at the Senate.

"Serving in Congress is an honor, not a side hustle," Sen. Bernie Moreno (R-OH), who introduced the resolution, posted on X after the vote. "Americans deserve to know that their leaders are here for the right reason!"

Polymarket (POLYMARKET) said it is in full support of this. "Our Rulebook & Terms of Service already prohibit such conduct, but codifying this into law is a step forward for the industry," it added.

"Kalshi (KALSHI) already proactively blocks members of Congress and enforces against insider trading," its CEO Tarek Mansour said. "This is a great step to increase trust in our markets by making it an industry standard. Now, let's pass this in the House!"

The Senate ban comes a week after a U.S. soldier involved in the capture of Nicolás Maduro was charged with using classified information to make $400,000 by betting on the Venezuelan leader's ouster.

Earlier this month, the White House warned staffers against using confidential, privileged information to trade on prediction markets.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Friday: TWLO, ROKU, RBLX, and more
data.description:
Stock futures showed mixed performance Friday morning as investor concerns mounted over persistent Middle East supply disruptions from the prolonged U.S.-Iran war.

Here are some of Friday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Atlassian Corporation (TEAM) +24% - Shares jumped after reporting strong FQ3 results, with adjusted EPS of $1.75 beating estimates and revenue rising 32% Y/Y to $1.79B, ahead of expectations. Cloud revenue grew 29% to $1.13B, while data center revenue surged 44% to $560.7M, reflecting strong enterprise demand. Looking ahead, the company guided Q4 revenue to $1.653B–$1.661B, roughly in line with estimates, with cloud growth expected at ~26.5% Y/Y. Atlassian also expects a gross margin of ~84.5% and an adjusted gross margin of ~88%, indicating continued profitability strength.
  • Twilio (TWLO) +18% - Shares surged after reporting strong Q1 results, with revenue rising 20% Y/Y to $1.41B, beating estimates and marking its fastest growth since 2022. Looking ahead, the company guided Q2 revenue to $1.42B–$1.43B, above expectations, with adjusted EPS of $1.27–$1.32, roughly in line. Twilio also raised its FY2026 outlook, increasing non-GAAP operating income and free cash flow ranges to $1.08B–$1.10B.
  • Reddit (RDDT) +12% - Shares gained following a strong Q1 beat, with revenue surging 69% Y/Y to $663M, driven by a 74% jump in advertising revenue. Adjusted EPS came in at $1.01, up 74% Y/Y and well above estimates, while user metrics remained solid, with global DAUs rising 17% to 126.8M and ARPU increasing 44% to $5.23. Looking ahead, the company guided Q2 revenue to $715M–$725M, with the midpoint above expectations, signaling continued momentum in both advertising and user growth.
  • Roku (ROKU) +7% - Shares rose after reporting strong Q1 results, with revenue up 22% Y/Y to $1.25B, beating estimates, driven by a 28% increase in platform revenue and growth in ads and subscriptions. The company also reported record premium subscriptions, with total new subscriptions rising 23% and streaming hours increasing 8% to 38.7B. Profitability improved sharply, with adjusted EBITDA up 165% to $85.7M and EPS turning positive at $0.57. Looking ahead, Roku expects Q2 revenue of $1.3B and ~20% platform revenue growth, while raising its full-year platform revenue outlook to +21% and maintaining total revenue guidance of ~$5.5B.

Biggest stock losers

  • Roblox (RBLX) -21% - Shares plunged after reporting mixed Q1 results, with revenue rising 39% Y/Y to $1.4B and bookings up 43% to $1.7B, both slightly below estimates. The company posted a wider loss of $0.35 per share, as higher costs and increased developer payouts weighed on profitability. User metrics also disappointed, with DAUs at 132M and hours engaged at 31B, both below expectations, partly impacted by safety-related changes and platform restrictions. Looking ahead, Roblox guided Q2 revenue to $1.39B–$1.45B and cut FY2026 revenue outlook to $5.878B–$6.14B, well below consensus, while lowering bookings growth expectations.
  • Western Digital Corporation (WDC) -8% - Shares dipped after reporting FQ3 results, despite beating expectations, with adjusted EPS of $2.72 and revenue rising 45% Y/Y to $3.34B, while the adjusted gross margin of 50.5% also came in above estimates. The company guided Q4 revenue to around $3.65B with a gross margin of 51%–52% and an adjusted EPS midpoint of $3.25, all above consensus, and announced a 20% increase in its quarterly dividend to $0.15 per share.

data.title: Zeta forecasts $1.785B 2026 revenue and $235M free cash flow as Athena expands enterprise adoption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zeta Global (ZETA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered our 19th consecutive beat and raise quarter" and said "AI is no longer a feature" and is "driving a replacement cycle" as enterprises demand "fewer systems, measurable results and applied intelligence that works today" (CEO David Steinberg).
  • "First quarter revenue of $396 million" and "adjusted EBITDA was $66 million" were paired with the view that "Athena by Zeta is an accelerant" and that "beta customers plus strong early adoption of Athena" contributed to the quarter’s revenue outperformance (CEO David Steinberg).
  • "After raising the midpoint of our range for 2026 revenue guidance last quarter by $25 million, we are again raising it by $30 million" as the company framed "market share gains" tied to "legacy Marketing Cloud replacement" and vendor consolidation (CEO David Steinberg).
  • "We also saw free cash flow conversion improved to 63%, generating $42 million in free cash flow" and "these results surpassed even our internal stretch goals" at "$26 million or 7 points above the midpoint of our revenue guidance" for the quarter (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Outlook

  • "For the full year 2026, we're increasing the midpoint of our revenue guidance by $30 million to $1.785 billion" and "increasing the midpoint of our 2026 guidance to $397 million" of adjusted EBITDA, plus "increasing our 2026 free cash flow guidance to $235 million" (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • "For the second quarter, we now expect revenue of $420 million at the midpoint" and "we now expect adjusted EBITDA of $86.6 million at the midpoint" (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • "None of our guidance increase is related to political candidate revenue" and "we continue to assume" political candidate revenue "will be $15 million in 2026" with "$7 million in the third quarter and $8 million in the fourth quarter"; guidance also assumed "minimal contribution from Athena" and a "conservative view of Marigold, contributing $47.5 million per quarter" for the rest of 2026 (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Financial Results

  • "First quarter revenue of $396 million" and "adjusted EBITDA was $66.1 million at a margin of 16.7%"; management said cost dynamics included "GAAP cost of revenue" at "41%" as "new agency wins" lifted "a higher initial mix of social as a channel" (CEO David Steinberg; CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • "GAAP net loss was $13.2 million" and "GAAP loss per share was $0.06"; management attributed the quarter’s EPS profile to "forecasted costs related to the integration of Marigold" as "the primary driver and not seen as recurring over the rest of the year" (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Cash and capital actions included "net cash provided by operating activities was $49.7 million" and "free cash flow of $41.7 million"; the company also said "we repurchased 1.5 million shares for $25.7 million" and had "approximately $138 million remaining" on the authorization (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Q&A

  • David Hynes, Canaccord Genuity: asked if Zeta benefited from competitive issues around The Trade Desk and whether pricing transparency could shift dynamics; CEO David Steinberg responded "the horse is out of the barn" for platforms "that do not have proprietary data" and said Zeta is "taking meaningful market share" and "we are going to continue to beat them handily in the marketplace."
  • David Hynes, Canaccord Genuity: asked what financial indicators will show Athena is "moving the needle"; CFO Christopher Greiner cited "a 7x increase" in "agentic interactions" in Athena’s first week of GA, and said "60% of the AI usage on our platform" was driven by Athena, tying impact to "ARPU expansion" and growth in "multiple use case customers" and "4 or more channels."
  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: asked how Zeta changes user behavior to drive Athena adoption; CEO David Steinberg said Athena was available to "100% of our enterprise clients" and that "Our top 30 clients have been onboarded," with "weekly learning and training" and an "ask Athena question of the week" program.
  • Gabriela Borges, Goldman Sachs: asked about inference costs and token usage; CEO David Steinberg said "The vast majority of our queries... are done on our own platforms" and "we are not buying tokens" for customer rollout, while internal tooling "Spade" and "Zippi" supported "75% automated new code creation."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were positive and adoption-focused, emphasizing competition, monetization signals, and pipeline velocity, including "what are the signs" Athena is "moving the needle" and whether it can "close new deals quicker" (David Hynes; Terrell Tillman, Truist).
  • Management tone was confident in product differentiation and guidance posture, using phrases such as "Could not be happier with this quarter" and "we are going to continue to beat them handily" while also emphasizing "maintaining our typical conservatism" in guidance (CEO David Steinberg; CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Compared with Q4 2025, the prepared tone moved from pre-launch anticipation ("remain on track to make Athena generally available by the end of the first quarter") to post-launch usage proof points ("7x increase" in agentic interactions; "60%" of AI usage driven by Athena) (CEO David Steinberg; CFO Christopher Greiner).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised again, shifting from "raising... by $25 million to $1.755 billion" (Q4) to "raising... by $30 million to $1.785 billion" (Q1) (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Athena narrative progressed from "remain on track to make Athena generally available" (Q4) to "Athena is currently available to all of our enterprise customers" with early GA usage metrics and deal anecdotes (CEO David Steinberg).
  • KPIs shifted from Q4 "184 super-scaled" customers and "$1.8 million" super-scaled ARPU to Q1 "189" super-scaled customers and "$1.7 million" super-scaled ARPU, with management highlighting Q1 ARPU growth at "21%" year-over-year (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Risks and Concerns

  • Margin pressure risk was tied to channel mix as "new agency wins" drove "a higher initial mix of social," which management said has "a higher cost of revenue"; mitigation relied on agencies scaling into "Zeta-owned channels" over time (CFO Christopher Greiner).
  • Integration execution risk remained centered on Marigold, with management saying it took "aggressive steps" on synergies expected to benefit margins "in Q2 and into the back half of the year" and describing Q1 integration costs as "not seen as recurring" (CFO Christopher Greiner).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as an Athena-driven acceleration in enterprise adoption and sales differentiation, while pairing the quarter’s $396 million revenue and $41.7 million free cash flow with another 2026 guide raise to $1.785 billion in revenue, $397 million in adjusted EBITDA, and $235 million in free cash flow, maintaining that the uplift excluded political candidate revenue and assumed minimal Athena contribution.
